∅: [zero (null) morpheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Null_morpheme) (such as fils-∅ (son-msg), with a 'zero' suffix for msg). Brackets may be used instead.
Ø: [zero (null) morpheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Null_morpheme) (such as fils-∅ (son-msg), with a 'zero' suffix for msg). Brackets may be used instead.
0: [zero (null) morpheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Null_morpheme) (such as fils-∅ (son-msg), with a 'zero' suffix for msg). Brackets may be used instead. or zeroth person ('[one](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/One_(pronoun))', as in Finnish, Keres) or epenthetic segment (semantically null)
4: [4th person](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Obviative) (= OBV) or 1st person inclusive or indefinite person
AB: from. May be equivalent to ABESS or ABL or [abessive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abessive_case) case (a.k.a. caritive case or privative case: 'without'). Lehmann (2004) recommends using privative (PRV) or aversive (AVERS) instead
ABESS: [abessive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abessive_case) case (a.k.a. caritive case or privative case: 'without'). Lehmann (2004) recommends using privative (PRV) or aversive (AVERS) instead
ABE: [abessive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abessive_case) case (a.k.a. caritive case or privative case: 'without'). Lehmann (2004) recommends using privative (PRV) or aversive (AVERS) instead
ABSV: [absentive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absentive) (occurring in a place displaced from the deictic centre)
ABSN: [absentive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absentive) (occurring in a place displaced from the deictic centre)
AC: motion across (as opposed to up/down-hill, -river) or animacy classifier or [accusative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accusative_case)
AD: near, by. May be equivalent to ADESS or ALL or agent demotion or anti-deictic
AEQ: [aequalis (equalis) case](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aequalis) (like, as), equational particle, [equative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equative_construction) (adj in nominal clause)
AGR: [agreement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agreement_(grammar)) affix (typically number–gender; cf. PNG). Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding and specifying agreement categories.
AG: [agentive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agentive_case) or [agreement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agreement_(grammar)) affix (typically number–gender; cf. PNG). Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding and specifying agreement categories.
AN: [animate gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animacy) (cf R; may exclude human referents) or action noun, action nominalizer or adnominalizer
ANT: [anterior tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anterior_tense) (relative tense; used for PRF in some traditions) or [antecedent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antecedent_(grammar)) or anticipated (future), anticipating
ANTE: in front of. May be equivalent to ANTESS or ANTL.
AP: adverbial particle [note: better to gloss the actual meaning]
ASC: [associative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Associative_case) ('with', 'à'; not = COM) or [associative plural](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_plural&action=edit&redlink=1) or [associative moodd](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_mood&action=edit&redlink=1).MOT associated motion
ASSOC: [associative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Associative_case) ('with', 'à'; not = COM) or [associative plural](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_plural&action=edit&redlink=1) or [associative moodd](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_mood&action=edit&redlink=1).MOT associated motion
ASSC: [associative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Associative_case) ('with', 'à'; not = COM) or [associative plural](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_plural&action=edit&redlink=1) or [associative moodd](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_mood&action=edit&redlink=1).MOT associated motion
ASS: [associative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Associative_case) ('with', 'à'; not = COM) or [associative plural](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_plural&action=edit&redlink=1) or [associative moodd](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Associative_mood&action=edit&redlink=1).MOT associated motion
ASP: [aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aspect_(grammar)), aspectual. Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding 'aspect' as a gloss and specifying the aspect.
AT: at (locative) [English preposition as a gloss] or [atelic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telicity)
ATR: [attributive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attributive) (l.atr attributive derived from place name), attributor
ATTR: [attributive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attributive) (l.atr attributive derived from place name), attributor
AUG: [augmentative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augmentative) or [augment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augment_(Bantu_languages)) (in Bantu noun classes) or augmented number (e.g. of imperative)
AUX: [auxiliary verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auxiliary_verb). Per Lehmann (2004), this should only be used if it uniquely identifies the morpheme (i.e., there is only one auxiliary morpheme in the language.)
BE: ['be' verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Be_verb) (a conflation of EXIST and COP) [cf. COP] or [copula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copula_%28linguistics%29), copulative
TB: ['be' verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Be_verb) (a conflation of EXIST and COP) [cf. COP]
C: [common gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_gender) or current evidence or conceptualizer or 'compass' in languages where relative position is based on [cardinal direction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardinal_direction) or complementizing (prefix on case abbreviation) or [converb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Converb)
CARD: cardinal numeral (morpheme or grammatical feature) or cardinal pronoun
CHEZ: at X's place, at the home of (from the French preposition [chez](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/chez))
CIRC: circumstantive ('in', 'by') or circumstantial voice (= CV) or 2nd element of a [circumfix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circumfix) or [circumferential](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Circumferential&action=edit&redlink=1)
CIRCUM: circumstantive ('in', 'by') or circumstantial voice (= CV) or 2nd element of a [circumfix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circumfix)
CV: circumstantial voice or circumstantial/theme voice/focus/trigger or copula verbalizer or characteristic vowel or conveyance voice (cf. CV circumstantial voice)
CJ: [conjoint](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjoint) or conjunctive (interpropositional relation), conjunct person marking
CL: close link (necessary condition; temporal closeness) or clause-level or nominal class (in Bantu languages)
CLF: [classifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classifier_(linguistics)) (base or morpheme). Some distinguish CLF classifier from CL [class marker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_marker_(morphology)). The category of classifier should be specified.
CLASS: [classifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classifier_(linguistics)) (base or morpheme). Some distinguish CLF classifier from CL [class marker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_marker_(morphology)). The category of classifier should be specified.
CLFSR: [classifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classifier_(linguistics)) (base or morpheme). Some distinguish CLF classifier from CL [class marker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_marker_(morphology)). The category of classifier should be specified.
CM: conjugation marker or noun-class marker or concatenative marker
CMP: completive (completitive) [aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_aspect) – normally = PFV or [comparative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparative) or [complementizer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complementizer) (= SUBR)
COMP: completive (completitive) [aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_aspect) – normally = PFV or [comparative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparative) or [complementizer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complementizer) (= SUBR) or compassion or comparative case (unequal comparison)
CMT: commitment, committal or [comitative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comitative_case) ('together with', 'in the company of')
CONJ: [conjunction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjunction_%28grammar%29) or conjunctive (interpropositional relation), conjunct person marking or conjectural (evidential) (NCONJ negative conjectural)
CONS: consequential or [consecutive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consecutive); [concessive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concessive) or consecutive mood ('so that')
CTR: [counterfactual conditional](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Counterfactual_conditional), contrafactuality or [contrastive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contrast_%28linguistics%29), contranstive focus, contrasted topic or control
CNTR: [contrastive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contrast_%28linguistics%29), contranstive focus, contrasted topic or continuer or counter-assertive
CONT: [contrastive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contrast_%28linguistics%29), contranstive focus, contrasted topic or [continuous aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continuous_and_progressive_aspects), [continuative aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continuative_aspect) or on a vertical surface. (From English contact.) May be equivalent to CONTESS or CONTL(AT) or continuous direction or [contentive](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Contentive&action=edit&redlink=1) or [contemporative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjunctive_participle) (at that/the same time)
CON: [contrastive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contrast_%28linguistics%29), contranstive focus, contrasted topic or [conative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conative) or [concrete](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abstract_and_concrete) or [conditional mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conditional_mood) ('if', 'would') (GCOND given conditional, GCCOND given concessive conditional) or connector
CONC: [concessive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concessive) ('although') (> CONCP concessive particle) or concurrent or concord marker [to be avoided in favor of specifying the agreement]
COND: [conditional mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conditional_mood) ('if', 'would') (GCOND given conditional, GCCOND given concessive conditional)
CND: [conditional mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conditional_mood) ('if', 'would') (GCOND given conditional, GCCOND given concessive conditional)
CRS: current relevance marker, currently relevant state (as in the [perfect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfect_%28grammar%29))
DE: [different event](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Different-subject_marker), change of event (cf. DS) or discontinued event or dual exclusive
D: [deixis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deixis), deictic or [demonstrative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demonstrative) or dependent, dependent clause marking (use SJV) or [determiner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Determiner) or [discourse marker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discourse_marker) or [distal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distal), distant or [dual number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dual_%28grammatical_number%29)
DEL: [delative case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delative_case) ('off of', 'down from') or [delimiter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delimiter), delimitative ('just, only'), delimiting or [deliberative mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deliberative_mood)
DIR: [direct evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) or directive, directional; typically suffixed to another element or [direct case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Direct_case) or direct voice (opposite of INV) or directed
DR: [direct evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) or [direct case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Direct_case) or downriver or different reference
DIREC: directive, directional (= LAT); typically suffixed to another element such as AD-, POST-, SUB-, SUPER-.
DIS: [dislocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dislocative_case) or [disjunction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logical_disjunction), disjunctive, disjunct person marking or [distal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distal), distant
DM: [discourse marker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discourse_marker) or demonstrative marker or directive marker (polite command)
DS: [distal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distal), distant or [different-subject](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Switch-reference)/actor/agent (change of subject) marker (cf. DE)
E: (used to form various [essive cases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Essive_case)) or [emphatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emphasis_%28linguistics%29) (e.g. emphatic base of pronouns), emphasizer, emphatic marker (ETOP emphatic topic) or [epenthetic morpheme, epenthetical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epenthesis) or [exclusive person](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clusivity)
EM: extension marker or evaluative marker or [emphatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emphasis_%28linguistics%29) (e.g. emphatic base of pronouns), emphasizer, emphatic marker (ETOP emphatic topic) or [epistemic mood or modality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epistemic_modality)
EMP: [emphatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emphasis_%28linguistics%29) (e.g. emphatic base of pronouns), emphasizer, emphatic marker (ETOP emphatic topic)
EMPH: [emphatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emphasis_%28linguistics%29) (e.g. emphatic base of pronouns), emphasizer, emphatic marker (ETOP emphatic topic)
FP: clause-final particle ([joshi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joshi_%28particle%29)) or [future participle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_participle) or far past
EV: [evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) [per Lehmann (2004), the particular evidential should be specified] or euphonic vowel or experiencer voice
EVD: [evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) [per Lehmann (2004), the particular evidential should be specified]
EVI: [evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) [per Lehmann (2004), the particular evidential should be specified]
EVID: [evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) [per Lehmann (2004), the particular evidential should be specified]
EXC: excessive [cf. EXESS 'ex-essive', which is commonly misspelled 'excessive'] or [exclusive person](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clusivity) or [exclamative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exclamation), exclamatory
XS: excessive [cf. EXESS 'ex-essive', which is commonly misspelled 'excessive']
EXP: experiencer, experiencer case or experiential, eyewitness = direct [evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) (cf. WIT) or [expletive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syntactic_expletive) (dummy / meaningless form)
EXPER: experiencer, experiencer case or experiential, eyewitness = direct [evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) (cf. WIT)
EXT: extended ([aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_aspect), demonstrative), extendible; extension (sound stretch) or extent or external evidential
FACT: [factive evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality)/mood, factual or factitive or [factative tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factative) (PRES if stative, PAST if not)
FAM: familiar, as for familiar [register](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Register_%28sociolinguistics%29) (as the [T–V distinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%25E2%2580%2593V_distinction)) and familiar pronominal
FOC: [focus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Focus_%28linguistics%29) (confusingly used both for [symmetrical voice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetrical_voice) and for true [grammatical focus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Focus_%28linguistics%29))
FOR: formal [register](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Register_%28sociolinguistics%29) (as the [T–V distinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%25E2%2580%2593V_distinction)) or formal mood or formal case ('in the capacity of...')
FRM: formal [register](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Register_%28sociolinguistics%29) (as the [T–V distinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%25E2%2580%2593V_distinction)) or formal mood or formal case ('in the capacity of...')
FORM: formal [register](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Register_%28sociolinguistics%29) (as the [T–V distinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%25E2%2580%2593V_distinction)) or formal mood or formal case ('in the capacity of...')
FRML: formal [register](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Register_%28sociolinguistics%29) (as the [T–V distinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%25E2%2580%2593V_distinction)) or formal mood or formal case ('in the capacity of...')
G1: [gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_gender) / noun class (e.g. G4 = 4th gender; may be used alongside M, F etc.)
G2: [gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_gender) / noun class (e.g. G4 = 4th gender; may be used alongside M, F etc.)
G3: [gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_gender) / noun class (e.g. G4 = 4th gender; may be used alongside M, F etc.)
G4: [gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_gender) / noun class (e.g. G4 = 4th gender; may be used alongside M, F etc.)
GND: [gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_gender) / noun class (e.g. G4 = 4th gender; may be used alongside M, F etc.)
GEN: [genitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genitive_case), genitive form of pronoun or generic, general
GO&: associated motion. GO&DO (go to a place and perform the verb)
AM: associated motion. GO&DO (go to a place and perform the verb)
DK: associated motion. GO&DO (go to a place and perform the verb)
H: [head](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_%28linguistics%29) or hearer/reader or [high variety/code](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diglossia), in a diglossic situation or [human, anthropic gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animacy) (cf. R) or higher animacy, higher object (cf. LA) or [honorific](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honorifics_%28linguistics%29) (subject honorific)
HOR: horizon of interest or horizontal or [hortative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hortative) (1st-person imperative)
IA: involuntary agent or indirect agent(ive) or instrumental advancement
IC: involuntary causative (natural or accidental events) or indirective copula or imperfective converb
IM: interrogative marker or immediate; immediate evidential or [imperfect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperfect)
IMMED: immediate; immediate evidential or immediate past
IN: in a container. May be equivalent to INESS or INL or [inclusive person](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clusivity) or [inessive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inessive_case) ('in')
INC: increment or [inceptive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchoative_aspect) (= inchoative or ingressive) or [inchoative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchoative_aspect) or [inclusive person](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clusivity) or incompletive/noncompletive [aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_aspect)
INCP: [inceptive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchoative_aspect) (= inchoative or ingressive) or incipient (INCPA athematic incipient, INCPT thematic incipient)
IP: [inceptive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchoative_aspect) (= inchoative or ingressive) or immediate past.
INCIP: incipient (INCPA athematic incipient, INCPT thematic incipient)
INT: [interjection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interjection) (incl. 'filler'), interjective or [interrogative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interrogative) or internal evidential or intentional conditional, intentive future or [intensifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intensifier), intensive
INTER: [interjection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interjection) (incl. 'filler'), interjective or [interrogative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interrogative) or within (a solid object). May be equivalent to INTERESS or INTERL.
INTEN: intentional conditional, intentive future or [intensifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intensifier), intensive
INVN: [inverse number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse_number) (as in Kiowa: sg of default pl, pl of default sg)
IPS: impersonal passive (passive w/o promotion to subject) or [impersonalizer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impersonal_verb) (AGIPS agent impersonalizer)
ITR: [iterative aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iterative_aspect) or intransitive (covers an [intransitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intransitive_case) for the S argument, = NOMS)
L: (used to form various [lative cases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lative_case)) or [low variety/code](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diglossia), in a diglossic situation or local (exophoric) person (= 1/2)
LL: land gender or lower level (spatial deixis)
LM: landmark or linking morph, linking interfix
LNK: linker, linking element: an [interfix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interfix) or a [ligature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligature_%28grammar%29)
LK: linker, linking element: an [interfix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interfix) or a [ligature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligature_%28grammar%29)
LINK: linker, linking element: an [interfix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interfix) or a [ligature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligature_%28grammar%29)
LV: locative/location voice/focus/trigger or linking vowel or lengthened vowel
M: [masculine gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Masculine_gender) or [modal case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modal_case) (prefix on case abbreviation) or marked or [middle voice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voice_%28grammar%29%23Middle), [mediopassive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediopassive_voice)
MED: [mediative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediative_case) or medial or middle voice (= MID)
MOD: [modal case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modal_case) (modalis), e.g. certainty or [mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_mood), [modal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linguistic_modality), modal particle or [modifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_modifier)
MSAP: main speech-act participant (= 1st person in assertions, 2nd in questions)
N: [neuter gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuter_gender). Sometimes = non-human or [noun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noun) or non-, in-, un-, a- or name
NTR: neutral aspect or intransitive (covers an [intransitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intransitive_case) for the S argument, = NOMS)
NF: non-final form/marker (cf. non-feminine) or non-finite (cf. non-feminine) or non-finite ([nonfinite verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonfinite_verb), [non-finite clause](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-finite_clause)) (NF may be ambiguous with non-feminine)
NFIN: non-finite ([nonfinite verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonfinite_verb), [non-finite clause](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-finite_clause)) (NF may be ambiguous with non-feminine)
NOMS: S-only nominative (S case in tripartite system, = NTR)
NP: noun particle (cf. NP 'noun phrase') or near past
INTR: intransitive (covers an [intransitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intransitive_case) for the S argument, = NOMS)
INTRANS: intransitive (covers an [intransitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intransitive_case) for the S argument, = NOMS)
O: object(ive) or [oblique case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oblique_case), oblique form of pronoun
OR: orientation (direction) marker or open reference (not specifically DS or SS)
OTHER: non-main speech-act participant (= 2nd or 3rd person in assertions, 1st or 3rd in questions)
P: pre-, post- or proper article or previous (evidence) or possessor or personal; personal/proper [article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Article_%28grammar%29); 'personal' affix (= 4th person) or [plural](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plural) or [past tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Past_tense) or [participle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle), [participial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle) (mood)
PSS: [passive voice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_voice) or previous event, same subject of v.i. (PSA previous same agent); PSSI and PSST previous event, same subject of v.i. and v.t.
PAT: [patientive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patient_%28grammar%29) (= UND). Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding, as it is not the value of a morphological category.
PC: [paucal number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paucal) or past completive or concord particle or perfective converb
PCL: 'polysemic clause linkage marker' or [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer
PCP: completive participle or participatory evidence or [participle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle), [participial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle) (mood)
PE: perpetuity or previous event or plural exclusive
PI: plural inclusive or yes–no question, polar question/interrogative
PR: [proper noun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proper_noun)/name, personal name or pragmatic or prior, preceding or [present tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Present_tense)
PO: [primary object](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Object_%28grammar%29) or patient-orientated verb or [possessive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Possession_%28linguistics%29), possessor or [postlocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Postlocative) (behind). May be equivalent to POSTESS or POSTL.
POST: [postlocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Postlocative) (behind). May be equivalent to POSTESS or POSTL or [postposition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Preposition_and_postposition), [postpositional case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Postpositional_case) or post-terminal aspect
PP: predicative possessive particle or [present progressive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Present_continuous) or [past perfect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pluperfect) or pragmatic particle or [passive participle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle), [past participle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle)
PREC: [precative mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precative_mood) (requests) or precondition (PRECA athematic precondition, PRECT thematic precondition) or precise, precision
PRECON: precondition (PRECA athematic precondition, PRECT thematic precondition)
PRT: [preterite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Preterite) or [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer or [partitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partitive_case)
PROP: [proprietive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proprietive_case) (quality of having X) or proper-noun marker or [propositive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Propositive_mood) (inclusive jussive)
PS: pseudo or passing state or [passé simple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pass%25C3%25A9_simple) or undergoer (patient-role subject) or [past tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Past_tense)
PSA: previous same agent of v.t. (PSS previous same subject)
PST.PR: past/present (different readings on different word classes)
PT: potent case inflection or [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer or [patient/object voice/focus/trigger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patient_voice)
PTCL: [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer
PTC: [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer
PTL: [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer
PART: [particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_particle) (Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and instead translating/glossing the meaning.), particalizer or [participle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle), [participial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Participle) (mood) or [partitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partitive_case)
PV: [patient/object voice/focus/trigger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patient_voice) or [pivot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syntactic_pivot) form/nominal or possessive verbalizer or [preverb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Preverb)
QM: quantity marker or question marker (usually = Q)
R: [rational gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animacy) (thinking beings) or reflexive or relational (prefix on case abbreviation) or [realis mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Realis_mood) or recent, recent past or reflexive ([reflexive pronoun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reflexive_pronoun)/[reflexive voice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reflexive_verb); 'R' used with person-number-gender) or relative clause marker or [relativizer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_clause) or relative pronoun affix or relational or relative case (possessor + A role) or reflexive/reciprocal
REC: recent, recent past or receptive or [reciprocal voice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reciprocal_construction)
RED: [reduplication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduplication), reduplicant (avoid if possible; instead gloss with meaning of reduplicated element)
RDP: [reduplication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduplication), reduplicant (avoid if possible; instead gloss with meaning of reduplicated element)
REDUP: [reduplication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduplication), reduplicant (avoid if possible; instead gloss with meaning of reduplicated element)
RDPL: [reduplication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduplication), reduplicant (avoid if possible; instead gloss with meaning of reduplicated element)
DUP: [reduplication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduplication), reduplicant (avoid if possible; instead gloss with meaning of reduplicated element)
REF: referential, referentive or reflexive ([reflexive pronoun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reflexive_pronoun)/[reflexive voice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reflexive_verb); 'R' used with person-number-gender)
REG: regal (e.g. pronouns) or regularity or regressive
REL: relative clause marker or [relativizer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_clause) or relative pronoun affix or relational or relative case (possessor + A role) or
RM: remote [past tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Past_tense) or remote [future tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_tense) or relative marker
REP: [repetitive aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iterative_aspect) (cf ITER), repetitive numeral, repeated word in repetition or [reported evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) (= HSY); reportative
REPET: [repetitive aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iterative_aspect) (cf ITER), repetitive numeral, repeated word in repetition
RPT: [repetitive aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iterative_aspect) (cf ITER), repetitive numeral, repeated word in repetition or [reported evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) (= HSY); reportative
RES: [resultative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resultative) or resignative or resumptive marker, [resumptive pronoun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resumptive_pronoun) or restrictive ([restrictive numeral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Restrictive_numeral), adverbial)
RET: [retrospective](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retrospective) (recollection; synonym for 'perfect' in some traditions) (PRETRO past retrospective)
RETRO: [retrospective](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retrospective) (recollection; synonym for 'perfect' in some traditions) (PRETRO past retrospective)
ROOT: (empty tag to mark second element of a divided [root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_%28linguistics%29))
RP: recent past or remote past or reflexive-possessive
RPR: remote past reported or [reported evidential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evidentiality) (= HSY); reportative
S: subjective or [subject case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subject_case), subject agreement or [singular](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_number) or singular intransitive action
SA: speaker authority (cf. AA) or [same-subject](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Switch-reference)/actor/argument marker (cf SE)
SUBJ: [subject case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subject_case), subject agreement (NSBJ non-subject) or subjective, subjective speaker perspective or [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject')
SF: subject focus or stem formation or sentence-final marker
SIM: simultaneous aspect, simultaneity or [similative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Similative) (e.g. plural based on prototypical member of group)
SIMV: [similative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Similative) (e.g. plural based on prototypical member of group)
SML: [similative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Similative) (e.g. plural based on prototypical member of group)
SJV: [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject')
SBJV: [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject')
SUBJV: [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject')
SUB: [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject') or [sublocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sublative_case) (under). May be equivalent to SUBESS or SUBL or [subitive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subitive) or [subordinator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subordination_%28linguistics%29) ('that'), subordinate
SB: [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject')
SU: [subjunctive mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjunctive_mood) (SUB and SUBJ may be ambiguous with 'subject')
SP: specific, specifying or sentence particle (= FP). See usage note at particle and FP or simple past, perfective past or subject prefix or speaker
SPC: specific, specifying (NSP, NSPC, NSPEC nonspecific: cf. also NSP entry)
SPEC: specific, specifying (NSP, NSPC, NSPEC nonspecific: cf. also NSP entry) or [specifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Specifier_%28linguistics%29) or [speculative mood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speculative_mood)
SPCF: specific, specifying (NSP, NSPC, NSPEC nonspecific: cf. also NSP entry)
SPECFC: specific, specifying (NSP, NSPC, NSPEC nonspecific: cf. also NSP entry)
SR: same referent or switch reference or [subordinator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subordination_%28linguistics%29) ('that'), subordinate
SSS: same-subject succession ('then') or simultaneous event, same subject (SSSI of intransitive clause, SSST of transitive clause)
ST: [stative aspect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stative_verb), [stative verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stative_verb) or (empty tag to mark second element of a divided [stem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem))
STEM: (empty tag to mark second element of a divided [stem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem))
SUP: [supine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supine) or [superlative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superlative) (most: cf. super-lative, super-essive) or [supplicative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supplicative), supplication or [superlocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superlocative). May be equivalent to SUPERESS or SUPERL or [superessive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superessive_case) ('above'; 'on')
SUPER: [superlocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superlocative). May be equivalent to SUPERESS or SUPERL or [superessive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superessive_case) ('above'; 'on')
SPR: [superlocative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superlocative). May be equivalent to SUPERESS or SUPERL or [super-lative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superlative), superdirective ('to above')
T: [trigger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trigger_%28linguistics%29) (used for AT, PT, GT etc.) [old fashioned; 'voice' is now standard] or thematic (TAMT thematic tense-aspect-mood, ANTT thematic antecedent, etc.) or temporal or [tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_tense); Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and specifying the tense.
TENS: number of tens (in a numeral) or [tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_tense); Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and specifying the tense.
TH: thematic element (e.g. thematic consonant, suffix); theme
THM: thematic element (e.g. thematic consonant, suffix); theme
THEM: thematic element (e.g. thematic consonant, suffix); theme
THEMAT: thematic element (e.g. thematic consonant, suffix); theme
TM: tense marker: HRS, TM DAYS, TM YRS for events hours, days, years ago
TNS: [tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_tense); Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and specifying the tense.
TS: [tense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_tense); Lehmann (2004) recommends avoiding this and specifying the tense or thematic suffix or tense
TR: [transitive verb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transitive_verb) (TRZ, TRR transitivizer); [transitive case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transitive_case) (rare) or transitional sound or trajector or [trial number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trial_number)
TRANSP: (transposition of deictic zero away from ego, e.g. 'uphill' from an object rather than from the speaker)
TRIP: retriplication [note: usually best to gloss with the meaning and ⟨~⟩]
UF: uncertain future or undergoer voice/focus/trigger (= { PV + LV + CV })
UR: upriver (cf UH away from the water)
USIT: [usitative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Usitative), for usual, customary or typical events
UV: undergoer voice/focus/trigger (= { PV + LV + CV }) or uncertain visual
VE: vegetable (food) gender. Some authors distinguish VE gender from VEG food affix.
VEG: vegetable (food) gender. Some authors distinguish VE gender from VEG food affix.
EQL: [aequalis (equalis) case](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aequalis) (like, as), equational particle, [equative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equative_construction) (adj in nominal clause)
EQTV: [aequalis (equalis) case](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aequalis) (like, as), equational particle, [equative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equative_construction) (adj in nominal clause)
